title: Virtual memory, mmap, overcommit, and the OOM killer
description: Allocations reserve virtual address space populated lazily by page faults; under overcommit the OOM killer reclaims by terminating a process.
body: Use when reasoning about memory footprint and OOM. Allocating or mmap'ing memory reserves virtual address space; physical pages (RSS) are only assigned when first touched, via page faults. Linux typically overcommits, so allocation can succeed even if the system cannot back it all; when physical memory and swap are exhausted, the kernel OOM-killer terminates a process chosen by a heuristic (often high-RSS), which may not be the one that caused the pressure. Do not assume a successful allocation means the memory is available. Actions: bound resident usage, stream large data, and watch RSS not just virtual size. Validation signals include flat RSS under load and no OOM events. Risk boundary: unbounded RSS growth risks killing this or a neighboring process; bound it and verify under sustained load.
